profile.summary: A Senior Engineer delivers complex technical work with limited oversight, owns important systems or features, and helps shape implementation choices. They balance hands-on delivery with code quality, design input, and support for less experienced engineers. They work closely with product, design, engineering peers, and their manager to turn goals into reliable software.

known_challenges.text: Balancing deep individual contribution with growing expectations to mentor others and review more work.
Owning delivery for complex projects while depending on other teams, unclear requirements, or shifting priorities.
Keeping technical quality high when deadlines push toward shortcuts and rework later.
Feeling pressure to be the person who can unblock everything, which can lead to overload.
Showing senior-level impact beyond coding, especially when promotion expectations are not clearly defined.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Scope and ownership of current work
recommended_question_themes.why: Senior engineers are often judged on how they handle ambiguous, high-impact work, not just task completion.
This role regularly has to balance speed, quality, maintainability, and risk in visible ways.
They often carry informal leadership work that can help the team but also drain focus from core delivery.
Delivery at this level often slows down because of coordination gaps rather than coding difficulty.
Senior engineers usually need clarity on what stronger system ownership and influence look like from here.

terminology.meaning: Checking code changes for quality, correctness, and maintainability.
Past shortcuts that make future changes slower or riskier.
Changing code structure without changing behavior.
High-level system design and how parts fit together.
A production problem affecting users or systems.
The underlying reason a problem happened.
Proposed code change for review before merging.
Automated build, test, and release pipeline.

listen_for: Whether they talk only about tickets finished or also about system impact and tradeoffs.
Repeated mentions of being a blocker remover for others.
Frustration about unclear ownership, changing requirements, or dependency delays.
Whether mentoring comes up as rewarding, invisible, or disruptive to their own work.
Shifts from confident technical language to vagueness when discussing longer-term growth.
avoid: Treating them like a pure delivery machine and ignoring mentoring, design input, and system ownership.
Assuming senior means they need little support or no career guidance.
Pushing them to take on every hard problem because they are reliable.
Using generic promotion advice without clarifying what senior success looks like on this team.
